title: Distributed Transactions Deep Dive — 2PC, 3PC, TCC & SAGA
description: Deep dive into distributed transaction protocols used at FAANG scale. Covers Two-Phase Commit (2PC), Three-Phase Commit (3PC), Try-Confirm/Cancel (TCC), and the SAGA pattern with choreography vs orchestration. Explains when each is appropriate, the failure modes, and how SAP's LUW (Logical Unit of Work) and RFC transaction handling provide a direct conceptual bridge.

description.value:
Overview: 2PC is the most widely known distributed transaction protocol. It involves a coordinator and multiple participants. The protocol has two phases:

Phase 1 — Prepare (Vote): (1) The coordinator sends a PREPARE message to all participants. (2) Each participant checks if it can commit the transaction locally — can it acquire all necessary locks? Is its local state consistent? (3) Each participant writes a 'prepare record' to its Write-Ahead Log (WAL): 'I prepared to commit transaction T.' This is a durable record of intent. (4) Each participant responds: YES (ready to commit) or NO (cannot commit). If any participant votes NO, the coordinator decides to abort.

Phase 2 — Commit/Abort (Decide): (1) If all participants voted YES, the coordinator writes a 'commit' record to its WAL and sends a COMMIT message to all participants. (2) If any participant voted NO, the coordinator writes an 'abort' record and sends an ABORT message to all participants. (3) Each participant receives the COMMIT/ABORT, writes the corresponding record to its WAL, releases locks, and sends an acknowledgment to the coordinator. (4) When the coordinator receives acknowledgments from all participants, it writes a 'complete' record to its WAL — the transaction is done.

Failure modes: (1) Participant failure during Phase 1: the coordinator times out and decides to abort. (2) Coordinator failure during Phase 1: participants are blocked — they hold locks indefinitely (the 'blocking problem'). A participant cannot decide whether to commit or abort without the coordinator. (3) Coordinator failure during Phase 2: participants who received the COMMIT message will commit. Participants who did not receive it are 'in doubt' — they have prepared but not received the decision. They remain blocked until the coordinator recovers. This is the fundamental problem with 2PC: it is a blocking protocol.

SAP Bridge: SAP's LUW (Logical Unit of Work) with update work process (V1/V2) is a 2PC-like protocol: the update function is prepared (V1) and then committed (V2). If the commit fails, the system can roll back. The key difference: SAP's LUW operates within a single database system; 2PC coordinates across independent database systems. SAP engineers understand the prepare-commit concept deeply — frame it as 'I understand the LUW commit protocol; now let me extend it across multiple independent systems.'

Why 3PC exists: 2PC's blocking problem — a coordinator failure leaves participants in doubt, holding locks indefinitely. 3PC adds a third phase to eliminate blocking, at the cost of an extra round-trip.

Phase 1 — CanCommit (Vote): The coordinator asks all participants: 'Can you commit this transaction?' Participants respond YES if they can guarantee they can prepare (no local issues). If any NO, the coordinator aborts. Unlike 2PC's PREPARE, this is a speculative check — participants do not make anything durable yet.

Phase 2 — PreCommit (Prepare): If all responded YES, the coordinator sends a PreCommit message: 'Prepare to commit, and be ready to commit.' Participants write the prepare record to their WAL and respond ACK. Now they are prepared and waiting for the final decision. The key difference from 2PC: the coordinator sets a timeout for PreCommit — if a participant doesn't receive the COMMIT within the timeout, it automatically aborts (releasing locks).

Phase 3 — DoCommit (Decide): When all participants have ACK'd PreCommit, the coordinator sends DoCommit. Participants commit and release locks. If a participant times out waiting for DoCommit, it aborts autonomously — it does NOT remain blocked waiting for the coordinator. This is what makes 3PC non-blocking: participants can independently abort after a timeout.

Why 3PC is rarely used in practice: (1) Three round-trips instead of two — adds latency. (2) More messages — larger network overhead. (3) It still cannot handle network partitions — if the coordinator is in one partition and participants in another, the participants will autonomously abort while the coordinator might commit. (4) 3PC assumes synchronous network (bounded delays), which real networks don't provide. In practice, most systems use 2PC with a high-availability coordinator (multiple replicas via Paxos/Raft) rather than 3PC.

Overview: TCC (Try-Confirm/Cancel) is a distributed transaction pattern designed for service-oriented architectures (SOA, microservices). Each participant exposes three operations: Try (reserve resources), Confirm (use the reserved resources), Cancel (release the reserved resources).

Try phase: (1) The coordinator calls the Try operation on each participant. (2) Each participant reserves the resources needed for the transaction. Example: Payment service reserves $50 on the user's credit card (authorization hold). Inventory service reserves 2 units of product X. Shipping service reserves a delivery slot. (3) If all Try operations succeed, the coordinator proceeds to Confirm. If any Try fails, the coordinator calls Cancel on all participants.

Confirm phase: (1) The coordinator calls Confirm on each participant. (2) Each participant finalizes the operation: Payment service captures the $50 (moves from authorization to settlement). Inventory service decrements the count. Shipping service confirms the delivery slot. (3) Confirm must be idempotent and designed to 'always succeed' — if it fails (rare), the coordinator retries. If it repeatedly fails, the system enters a manual resolution state (compensating transaction required).

Cancel phase (compensating): (1) The coordinator calls Cancel on each participant. (2) Each participant releases the reserved resources: Payment service releases the authorization hold. Inventory service un-reserves the units. Shipping service releases the delivery slot. (3) Cancel must be idempotent — it can be called multiple times safely. (4) Cancel is the 'compensating transaction' — it undoes what Try did.

TCC vs 2PC: TCC is often called 'application-level 2PC.' The difference: 2PC uses database locks (pessimistic); TCC uses business-level reservations (optimistic). TCC handles long-running transactions better because reservations can be released more flexibly (e.g., a credit card authorization hold expires after 7 days automatically). TCC is used by many payment systems (Stripe, Adyen) and e-commerce platforms (Alibaba's Seata framework implements TCC).

The SAGA pattern: A SAGA is a sequence of local transactions where each transaction publishes an event that triggers the next transaction. If a transaction fails, the SAGA executes compensating transactions in reverse order to undo the work of previous transactions. SAGAs are the most practical pattern for microservice transactions at FAANG scale.

Choreography-based SAGA: (1) Each service publishes domain events after completing its local transaction. (2) Other services subscribe to events and trigger their own transactions. (3) Example: Order Service creates an order and publishes 'OrderCreated' event. Payment Service listens, processes payment, publishes 'PaymentProcessed' event. Inventory Service listens, reserves inventory, publishes 'InventoryReserved' event. (4) If Payment Service fails, it publishes 'PaymentFailed' event. Order Service listens and cancels the order. (5) Pros: no central coordinator, natural event-driven flow, good for simple workflows. (6) Cons: complex to trace and debug, no central point for monitoring, hard to add new steps or change the flow.

Orchestration-based SAGA: (1) A central orchestrator (or SAGA execution coordinator) manages the flow. The orchestrator sends commands to each service and handles responses. (2) Example: Orchestrator sends 'CreateOrder' to Order Service, receives order_id, sends 'ProcessPayment' to Payment Service, receives payment_id, sends 'ReserveInventory' to Inventory Service. (3) If any step fails, the orchestrator calls compensating actions in reverse order: 'CancelPayment', 'CancelOrder'. (4) The orchestrator persists its state (current step, context) in a database — if the orchestrator crashes, it resumes from the last persisted state. (5) Pros: central visibility, easy to monitor and manage, simple to add error handling and retry logic. (6) Cons: the orchestrator is a potential bottleneck and single point of failure (mitigated by persistence + HA).

When to use each: Choreography for simple, linear workflows with few services (2-3). Orchestration for complex workflows with 4+ services, branching, error handling, and monitoring requirements. FAANGs use orchestration for mission-critical flows (order placement, account creation) and choreography for simpler internal workflows.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Distributed transactions are a core system design topic for Staff+ interviews, especially at Amazon (DynamoDB transactions), Google (Spanner), and Stripe. The key signals: (1) Understanding 2PC's blocking problem — this is the most important insight. (2) Comparing 2PC vs TCC vs SAGA — shows you understand the tradeoff space. (3) Choreography vs orchestration for SAGAs — practical knowledge that distinguishes Staff+ from Senior. SAP engineers with LUW experience have a unique advantage — you already understand prepare-commit semantics. Frame it as: 'I've worked with transaction protocols for 10+ years in SAP's enterprise systems; distributed transactions apply the same principles across independent services.'
